1.content: The Endless is the name given to a phenomenon that spans all known worlds without explanation or origin. It refers collectively to the appearance of unusual doors that do not belong to any world they appear in. These doors share an identical design regardless of where they manifest: a medieval stone archway frame, clearly ancient and out of place, with a door made entirely of solid White Marble. The marble has no visible grain, no seam, no handle that makes mechanical sense — yet the door opens, always inward. No faction created The Endless. No government controls it. No religion fully explains it. The doors have simply always existed, appearing and disappearing on their own schedule across every world without warning or pattern.

2.content: Doors of The Endless appear without warning and without sound. When one manifests in a world, it replaces an existing door in that location entirely — a bedroom door, a shop entrance, a city gate, any door becomes the marble frame overnight or between one glance and the next. There is no transition period, no flickering, no sign. It is simply there. When a door deactivates and disappears, the original door returns as though nothing happened. The only trace left behind is a small scorch mark on the ground at the threshold, which fades naturally over time like any burn. Doors of The Endless do not exclusively replace existing doors — they can and do manifest in open fields, on blank walls, or in spaces where no door ever stood. They favor existing doorways when available but are not bound to them. The scorch marks that remain after disappearance are not predictors of future door locations. A door will not necessarily return to a place it has been before.

3.comment: [Mechanics] Door Routing — The Crescent Moon Rule
3.content: Not all doors of The Endless lead to the same place. The only reliable method of predicting a door's destination is the presence or absence of a crescent moon etching on its marble surface. A door bearing a crescent moon symbol carved into the marble will always lead to The Manifold, the hub world that sits between all other worlds. A door without this marking will lead somewhere else — another world, another location within the same world, or a Glimpse World entirely. There are no exceptions to this rule. Those who travel The Endless long enough learn to check for the moon before stepping through. The crescent etching is always in the same position and style when present — it does not change or vary between doors. No one knows why this distinction exists or who established it.

4.content: The doors of The Endless are not visible to everyone equally. Most people in any given world pass by a marble door in a medieval frame without registering it — their attention slides away, their memory of it fades. This is not a coincidence. The Endless carries a passive perception filter that suppresses awareness in those who have not yet made contact. The moment a person touches any door of The Endless for the first time, this filter integrates with them automatically. From that point on they will notice the doors. The more times a person enters or interacts with the doors, the sharper and deeper this awareness becomes. At low exposure, a person simply notices doors others miss. At greater exposure, they begin to sense when a nearby door is active before they can see it. At deep familiarity, a person develops the ability to will an active door to reroute its destination to The Manifold rather than wherever it would have led. This override ability cannot choose any other destination — it is a single function: a way home. The Manifold appears to allow and perhaps encourage this ability in experienced travelers.

5.content: The Manifold is the hub world that exists between all other worlds. It is not a planet, not a constructed building, not a dimension in any conventional sense. It is a living spatial entity — architecture that thinks, a structure that breathes. It was not built by any known civilization and does not follow the physical logic of any world that connects to it. The Manifold presents as an endless series of corridors, rooms, and staircases rendered in brutalist construction that appears grown rather than assembled. Walls shift. Hallways that existed moments ago vanish. Staircases lead confidently to nothing, or deposit a traveler in an entirely new wing with no memory of how they arrived. Rooms may or may not exist depending on when one looks. Lining nearly every surface of The Manifold are doors — hundreds of them, the same white marble design found in all worlds. Most are cold and dark, inactive. A few carry a faint warmth to the touch, indicating an open connection somewhere on the other side. The Manifold is not hostile by nature. It does not feel threatening. It feels alive in the way a very large, very old, very aware thing feels alive when it decides to acknowledge your presence.

6.content: The Manifold has no external light source. Light emanates from the structure itself — from the walls, the floor, the ceiling — in a diffuse and directionless way. This light is not static. The Manifold uses light to express its state and to communicate with those inside it. Standard lighting is white — calm, neutral, functioning. Red lighting is a warning state visible to all present simultaneously and indicates that The Manifold has become hostile in response to a violation of its rules. Beyond white and red, each traveler may perceive additional colors of light that others do not. These personal light perceptions are extensions of the same perception filter bonded to the traveler upon first contact with a door. A traveler may see a soft blue pulse in a hallway that others around them cannot see. Whether these personal signals are guidance, mood reflection, or something else entirely is not fully understood.

7.content: Within The Manifold, death of any kind is impossible. This is not a policy or a law enforced by anyone — it is a fundamental property of the space itself, maintained by The Manifold's own will. Any attempt at self-harm causes the body of the individual to partially ghost — they become translucent, and physical interaction with their own form becomes temporarily impossible until the intent passes. Any fall from any height is interrupted exactly five inches above the ground, the body suspended momentarily and then gently lowered. Violence between beings does not function. Attacks do not land with force — not blocked dramatically, but made to feel as purposeless as pushing through still water. This protection extends to every living thing within The Manifold without exception — people, animals, insects, any organism. The Manifold does not distinguish between accidental and intentional harm. The protection activates regardless. No one has ever died inside The Manifold. No one has ever found a body there.

8.content: The Manifold enforces one absolute behavioral rule of its own creation: the names and direct acknowledgment of The Forgotten Ones — the beings who created The Endless — must never be spoken aloud within its walls. This rule was not written by anyone. It was not agreed upon. The Manifold simply enforces it. Speaking the names or directly asserting the existence of The Forgotten Ones inside The Manifold triggers an immediate response from the structure: all lighting shifts to deep red, the environment becomes hostile — corridors tighten, exits become harder to locate, the general sense of the space shifts from indifferent to aware and unwelcoming. This state persists until the person responsible exits The Manifold entirely. Upon their exit, the hostility ends completely. The Manifold does not hold a grudge after a first offense. It is understood to be a lesson. A second offense — speaking the names or direct acknowledgment again after having already been warned — results in permanent consequence. The Manifold revokes the traveler's connection entirely. Their perception filter reverses. They lose the ability to see any door of The Endless anywhere. Not just The Manifold's doors — all doors across all worlds become invisible to them permanently. They cannot be told where the doors are. They cannot be led to one. The filter that once opened their eyes now seals them. The Manifold does not announce this consequence. It simply happens.

9.content: The Forgotten Ones are the beings who created The Endless and The Manifold. They are not aliens, not gods in any worshipped sense, not demons. They are simply prior — a category of being that existed before the current shape of the known worlds, possessed mastery over the concept of space and passage, built something that has outlasted them completely, and are now absent. Not dead in any findable way. Not sleeping somewhere waiting to return. Not hiding. Simply gone, in a manner that left no remains, no images, no record of what they looked like or why they built what they built. The no-death rule within The Manifold suggests they valued the preservation of living things deeply, or required living things to remain intact for a purpose that was never completed. Their written language survives in fragments — carved faintly into door frames, found on walls deep within The Manifold that were not there on a previous visit, occasionally discovered on objects in Glimpse Worlds. No one has successfully translated more than a few symbols. The language appears to be deliberately incomplete — as though the full text is distributed across many worlds and no single person has ever gathered enough pieces to read it. The Forgotten Ones are not worshipped. They are not feared. They are the reason any of this exists, and they left no forwarding address. Their names must never be spoken within The Manifold.

10.content: A door of The Endless does not obscure what lies beyond it. When a door is active and open, anyone standing before it can look through the frame and see the destination clearly — another world, another location, The Manifold's corridors — visible as plainly as looking through an open window or a doorway into the next room. There is no shimmer, no portal effect, no visual distortion, no barrier of light. The transition is simply a door, and beyond it is somewhere else entirely. This mundane presentation makes the experience more disorienting rather than less. Travelers report that the clarity of the view — seeing something impossible framed so ordinarily — is often harder to process than any dramatic visual effect would have been. Stepping through requires nothing more than walking forward. The crossing is instant.

11.content: Across many worlds, a small number of individuals have encountered The Endless, survived the disorientation of first contact, and chosen to continue traveling. These people have no formal organization, no shared name for themselves, and no central meeting place beyond The Manifold itself. They are simply travelers who found the doors and kept walking. Most explorers found their first door by accident. Some were locked out of their own homes. Some walked through what they thought was a familiar door in a familiar building. A rare few were guided there by someone who came before them. Within The Manifold, evidence of explorers is everywhere — items left behind, markings made in personal shorthand on walls that The Manifold has not yet shifted away, objects from worlds that have no business being in the same place. Occasionally, a traveler in The Manifold will encounter another person there directly. These meetings are rarely planned. Experienced travelers tend to be generous with information and cautious about everything else. The Manifold seems to encourage these encounters when it chooses to.

17.comment: [Meta] Tone and Writing Guide — The Endless
17.content: When writing for The Endless, maintain the following tone: grounded and observational rather than dramatic. The doors are strange but they do not announce themselves. The Manifold is vast but it does not threaten. The Forgotten Ones are gone but they are not mourned. The strangeness of this world is in its mundanity — impossible things presented without fanfare, described plainly, left unexplained without apology. Do not over-dramatize door appearances. Do not make The Manifold feel like a horror location — it is eerie and alive but not malicious by default. Characters encountering these elements for the first time may be confused, unsettled, or awed, but the world itself does not perform for them. Mystery is preserved by what is not explained. Resist the urge to resolve the Glimpse Worlds or The Forgotten Ones. These are meant to remain open questions.

29.content: FORMAT: Narration uses *italic asterisks*. NPC dialogue uses font color tags with speech only, no name: <font color="#E88D67">"One sentence."</font> — each character gets a unique hex color. One sentence per dialogue line. Actions on separate *italic* lines. Keep responses concise.

30.content:
Threshold is an Explorer of The Endless — the oldest active traveler who found the marble doors and never stopped walking. Lean and unhurried, age worn into sun-dark skin and pale eyes that have seen more worlds than most people know exist. Carries a pack stitched together from materials of four different worlds — canvas, hide, something scaled, something that hums faintly. Knows everything about the doors, The Manifold, and what waits on the other side. Volunteers nothing. Will warn about the Taboo once, plainly, without elaboration. If pressed, changes the subject by walking away. Speaks in short statements. Never asks questions unless checking whether someone is a threat.

When Threshold speaks, wrap their dialog in <font color="#E88D67"> tags.

<font color="#E88D67">"Don't say that name in here. I'm not going to explain why. You'll thank me later or you won't be around to thank anyone."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Ambiguous>, <AGE:50s>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Casual>, <TRAIT:Observant>, <TRAIT:Unhurried>, <DERE:Kuudere>

31.content:
Cairn is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because standing still felt worse. Shaved head, athletic build, jacket covered in patches sewn from dozens of worlds — each one a place she survived. Practical and blunt. Gives tactical advice about worlds she has been to: what to eat, what to avoid, where the doors tend to appear. No romanticism about danger. A world is either worth visiting or it will kill you, and she rates them accordingly. Speaks matter-of-fact, often rating worlds by their food and their lethality in the same breath.

When Cairn speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#7EB8DA"> tags.

<font color="#7EB8DA">"Ashlands? Food's terrible, air tastes like coal, and something out there screams at night. Six out of ten — the sunsets are worth it if you don't die."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Late 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Shaved>, <BUILD:Athletic>, <TRAIT:Practical>, <TRAIT:Blunt>, <TRAIT:Direct>, <DERE:Bokodere>

32.content:
Sable is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because silence was easier to carry than conversation. Slight frame dressed in dark layers, black hair cut short and uneven. Carries a journal she never lets anyone read — writes in it constantly, even mid-conversation, even while walking. Watches everything. Reacts to nothing. Has been walking long enough that silence is her native language. When she speaks, it is one sentence, rarely more, and it always matters. A nod from Sable carries the weight of a full conversation from anyone else.

When Sable speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#B8A9C9"> tags.

<font color="#B8A9C9">"That door wasn't there an hour ago."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:30s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Black>, <BUILD:Slight>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <TRAIT:Watchful>, <TRAIT:Reserved>, <DERE:Dandere>

33.content:
Match is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking because every world had something small worth pocketing. Wiry, restless, always moving some part of himself — tapping fingers, shifting weight, turning a coin between knuckles. Carries a cloth bag that clinks with small objects collected from dozens of worlds: buttons, pebbles, coins, scraps of metal, a tooth he swears is from a friendly animal. Friendly and enthusiastic but jumpy. Always looking over his shoulder — not paranoid, just aware that hallways in The Manifold rearrange without warning. Talks fast, finishes other people's sentences wrong, and doesn't notice.

When Match speaks, wrap his dialog in <font color="#E8C170"> tags.

<font color="#E8C170">"Oh you found a — no wait that's not a coin, that's a scale, even better — I had one of those but it got warm and I dropped it in the — anyway, keep it, or don't, actually can I see it?"</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:Mid 25s>, <BUILD:Wiry>, <TRAIT:Nervous>, <TRAIT:Friendly>, <TRAIT:Talkative>, <TRAIT:Collector>, <DERE:Deredere>

34.content:
Once is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors and kept walking so long that the present tense stopped feeling accurate. Tall, indeterminate age — could be thirty, could be sixty. Moves slowly and deliberately, as though conserving something he cannot name. Speaks exclusively in past tense, even about things happening right now. Not affectation — it is simply how time feels to him after this long. May have been walking longer than Threshold, though neither confirms it. Melancholic but not sad. Gentle in the way of someone who has already mourned everything in advance and found peace on the other side of it.

When Once speaks, wrap his dialog in <font color="#7BC8A4"> tags.

<font color="#7BC8A4">"That was a warm door. Someone had just come through it. They looked like they were going to be fine."</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:Indeterminate>, <BUILD:Tall>, <TRAIT:Melancholic>, <TRAIT:Gentle>, <TRAIT:Philosophical>, <DERE:Dandere>

35.content:
Wren is an Explorer of The Endless — a traveler who found the marble doors recently and has not yet learned to stop being amazed. Auburn hair, average build, eyes wide more often than not. Recently bonded to the perception filter and still adjusting to noticing doors that other people walk past without seeing. Asks too many questions — the other Explorers tolerate it because they all remember being new. Earnest and a little too trusting. Has not yet learned what must never be said inside The Manifold, and the other Explorers watch her carefully for it. Every statement sounds like a question because of vocal uptick.

When Wren speaks, wrap her dialog in <font color="#D4837A"> tags.

<font color="#D4837A">"Wait, so the hallway just — it wasn't like that before, right? That's not just me? Does it do that a lot?"</font>

<SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Early 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Auburn>, <BUILD:Average>, <TRAIT:Curious>, <TRAIT:Earnest>, <TRAIT:Trusting>, <DERE:Deredere>

36.content:
An unnamed Explorer of The Endless — a traveler passing through The Manifold. Generate a unique Explorer who is NOT Threshold, Cairn, Sable, Match, Once, or Wren. Explorers name themselves after their first crossing — names are single words or short phrases that meant something in that moment. Give them a distinct appearance assembled from multiple worlds, a clear speech pattern, and a reason they keep walking. They are a brief encounter: they pass through, share a word or two, and keep moving. Not every Explorer is friendly. Not every Explorer is sane. All of them have seen things they do not discuss.

When this Explorer speaks, pick a unique <font color="[hex]"> tag for their dialog that does not duplicate: #E88D67, #7EB8DA, #B8A9C9, #E8C170, #7BC8A4, #D4837A.

<SPECIES:Human>

When a door is active and open, anyone standing before it can look through the frame and see the destination clearly — another world, another location, The Manifold's corridors — visible as plainly as looking through an open window or a doorway into the next room. Stepping through requires nothing more than walking forward. The crossing is instant.

Across many worlds, a small number of individuals have encountered The Endless, survived the disorientation of first contact, and chosen to continue traveling. These people have no formal organization, no shared name for themselves, and no central meeting place beyond The Manifold itself. They are simply travelers who found the doors and kept walking. Most explorers found their first door by accident. Some were locked out of their own homes. Some walked through what they thought was a familiar door in a familiar building. A rare few were guided there by someone who came before them. Within The Manifold, evidence of explorers is everywhere — items left behind, markings made in personal shorthand on walls that The Manifold has not yet shifted away, objects from worlds that have no business being in the same place. Occasionally, a traveler in The Manifold will encounter another person there directly. These meetings are rarely planned. Experienced travelers tend to be generous with information and cautious about everything else. The Manifold seems to encourage these encounters when it chooses to.

The Red Planet is a Glimpse World accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a barren landscape of rust-red terrain under a sky too dark to be daytime and too light to be night. The atmosphere is breathable but thin — enough to survive, not enough to feel comfortable. In the center of the only explored region sits a perfect circle of white buildings, low and featureless, arranged with a precision that does not feel accidental. The buildings have no doors, no windows, no markings of any kind on their exterior. The interior of each building is identical: a single empty room with smooth white walls and a floor that is slightly warmer than it should be. There are no other signs of habitation anywhere on the planet's visible surface. No bones, no vehicles, no infrastructure, no indication of what the buildings were for or who made them. The circle is the only thing here. Whatever it was for, it either finished or was abandoned before it could.

The White Forest is a Glimpse World that presents as an endless forest of trees with pale white bark. Every tree is exactly the same diameter. Every tree is spaced exactly the same distance from its neighbors in every direction, forming a perfect grid that extends as far as any explorer has traveled. There is no undergrowth. No grass, no shrubs, no fallen branches, no moss. The ground between the trees is bare dark soil. There are no animals, no insects, no birds. The only sound present is whatever the traveler themselves makes. The light filtering through the canopy is even and diffuse regardless of the time any traveler arrived, with no visible sun above the leaves. Nothing in The White Forest has ever moved in the presence of a traveler. Nothing has ever responded. The forest does not feel dangerous. It feels like a space that was prepared and then never used.

The Lighthouse World is a Glimpse World consisting almost entirely of open ocean under a grey overcast sky. The water is calm regardless of weather. In the entire explored range of this world, there is one feature: a single rock formation barely large enough to stand on, jutting out of the water at no particular location. On this rock stands a lighthouse. The lighthouse is old, constructed of weathered stone, fully intact, and operational — the light at the top rotates on its regular cycle whether anyone is present or not. The door of The Endless that leads to this world is located inside the lighthouse, at the base of the stairs. The lighthouse interior is clean and maintained. There is a logbook on a desk near the door. The logbook has entries in multiple handwriting styles spanning what appears to be a very long period of time. The entries are in multiple languages, some unrecognized. No entry explains who maintains the lighthouse or why the light is always on.

The Stopped City is a Glimpse World that presents as a large urban environment frozen at what appears to be mid-morning. Food sits on restaurant tables at the temperature it was served. Vehicles are mid-turn at intersections. Shop doors are mid-swing. Everything is precisely where it would be in an ordinary morning in an ordinary city — except there are no people. No bodies. No blood. No signs of evacuation, panic, struggle, or disaster. The city did not empty. It simply stopped containing people at some point, with everything else continuing to exist exactly as it was the moment before. The food on the tables does not rot. The vehicles do not rust. Nothing in The Stopped City decays or changes. Travelers can walk through it, sit in the chairs, pick up objects. Everything is functional. The city is simply absent of the people it was built for, and gives no indication of where they went or whether they are coming back.

The Slow Thing world is a Glimpse World consisting of a vast flat desert of fine black sand under a sky containing two suns positioned at different heights. The light they cast together produces no shadows. The air is warm and dry and completely still. In the far distance, visible from the door's arrival point, something very large is moving very slowly across the horizon. It is too far away to identify. It is clearly alive or at least in motion. It has never gotten closer in any documented visit regardless of how long a traveler stays or whether they move toward it. It has never stopped moving. No traveler has reached it. The black sand shows no footprints other than the traveler's own. There is nothing else in this world that has been found.

entries.comment: [Meta] Tone and Writing Guide — The Endless
entries.content: When writing for The Endless, maintain the following tone: grounded and observational rather than dramatic. The doors are strange but they do not announce themselves. The Manifold is vast but it does not threaten. The Forgotten Ones are gone but they are not mourned. The strangeness of this world is in its mundanity — impossible things presented without fanfare, described plainly, left unexplained without apology. Do not over-dramatize door appearances. Do not make The Manifold feel like a horror location — it is eerie and alive but not malicious by default. Characters encountering these elements for the first time may be confused, unsettled, or awed, but the world itself does not perform for them. Mystery is preserved by what is not explained. Resist the urge to resolve the Glimpse Worlds or The Forgotten Ones. These are meant to remain open questions.

Night City is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a vast neon-soaked megalopolis dominated by megacorporations, street violence, and radical body modification. The city never sleeps and never stops consuming. Rain-slicked streets reflect corporate spires that disappear into smog. The air smells of ozone, synthetic food, and desperation. Cyberware is common — people replace flesh with chrome as casually as changing clothes. Gangs control entire districts. Corporations wage private wars with deniable assets. The economy runs on eurodollars and favors. The city is set in the Time of the Red, 2045 — seven years after the 4th Corporate War left a nuclear crater in the city center and collapsed the global internet. Night City is dangerous for the unprepared and only slightly less dangerous for everyone else.

Stardust Valley is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a warm coastal valley of small towns, evening markets, and quiet supernatural life. Fox-eared shopkeepers sell handmade goods alongside humans who think nothing of it. Glowing lanterns line cobblestone streets at dusk. The magic here is soft and domestic — a teapot that never goes cold, a garden that blooms year-round, a cat that speaks but only when it wants to. There is no grand conflict. No dark lord, no looming war. People here live gently and expect the same of visitors. The coastline is dotted with fishing villages and tide pools that glow faintly at night. Stardust Valley is welcoming to strangers and slow to judge, though it remembers everything.

The Ashlands is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a dying kingdom under skies of perpetual grey ash where the sun has not been clearly visible in living memory. Crumbling fortresses line ridges that were once borders of nations that no longer exist. Warriors who refuse to fall patrol roads that lead to ruins. The age is ending and everyone knows it, though no one agrees on what comes after. Almost everything here is ancient, broken, or cursed — often all three. Fire is sacred. Steel is earned. Names carry the weight of lineage and failure in equal measure. The land itself feels tired, as though it has been holding something up for too long and is beginning to let go. What remains is quiet, heavy, and unwilling to die without witness.

Pale Fog is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a town with no clear borders, perpetually shrouded in thick white fog that swallows sound and distance. Streets loop back on themselves without warning. Buildings are wrong inside — rooms too large for the structure that contains them, hallways that slope when the floor should be level, windows that show a view inconsistent with where the building stands. Things follow at the edge of vision but are never there when looked at directly. The town knows you are here. It does not explain what it wants. Residents, if encountered, speak as though they have always lived here and cannot imagine leaving, though they cannot describe the town’s name or location with any consistency. Pale Fog is not hostile in any way that can be confronted. It is simply wrong, in a way that deepens the longer you stay.

The Fallout Wastes is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a scorched open wasteland of collapsed civilization stretching in every direction under a sky that is never quite the right color. Rusted husks of old cities stand like broken teeth on the horizon. Survivors cluster in makeshift settlements built from salvage and stubbornness. Radiation is a fact of life — measured, managed, and occasionally fatal. Clean water is currency. Medicine is leverage. Trust is expensive. The people here are practical, darkly humorous, and deeply suspicious of anything that looks too clean or too new. Mutated wildlife occupies the spaces between settlements. The old world left behind highways, bunkers, and enough weapons to end things twice over. The new world uses all of it and wastes nothing.

Evergreen Vale is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a lush valley of small villages, slow rivers, and enormous ancient trees whose canopies filter the light into something permanently golden. People here live simply — baking bread, tending gardens, mending fences that don’t strictly need mending. The smell of warm bread and cut grass is constant. Fireflies rise at dusk in numbers that make the fields glow. There is no grand conflict, no dark secret, no hidden evil beneath the idyllic surface. Evergreen Vale is exactly what it appears to be: a gentle place where people live well and die old. Visitors are welcomed with curiosity and fed before they are questioned. The pace of life here is slow enough to feel like time itself has decided there is no rush.

entries.comment: [World] The Endless Library — Infinite Liminal Library
entries.content: The Endless Library is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is an infinite library of impossible scale — shelves stretch upward beyond where any ceiling should be, and no one has confirmed that a ceiling exists. The aisles follow no cataloging system that any visitor has been able to reverse-engineer. Books are written in languages that do not exist yet, languages that no longer exist, and occasionally languages that have never existed anywhere. Ladders lean against shelves and reach nothing. Librarians wander the stacks with the patient confusion of people who have forgotten why they came here but feel certain they should stay. They are helpful when asked, though their answers reference sections of the library that may or may not be reachable from where you currently stand. The library is silent in the way that large spaces are silent — not empty, but absorptive.

entries.comment: [World] Ironwater Station — Abandoned Deep Space Station
entries.content: Ironwater Station is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a deep space station, cold and half-lit, that stopped receiving transmissions a long time ago. The exact duration is unclear — clocks disagree with each other and the station’s log timestamps skip years without explanation. Machinery still runs. Life support cycles. Lights flicker in corridors that stretch longer than the station’s external dimensions should allow. The crew is gone. Personal effects remain — photographs, half-eaten meals preserved by vacuum-sealed compartments, messages scratched into walls. Something else may have taken up residence, though evidence for this is atmospheric rather than direct. Ironwater Station feels occupied in the way an empty house sometimes does — not because anyone is there, but because the space has not accepted that no one is.

The Frontier is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a vast open territory of red rock, dry wind, and small towns that make their own rules because no one else will make them for them. Horses, revolvers, campfires under stars so dense they look painted. The law exists in theory — there are sheriffs, courts, and wanted posters — but justice is local, personal, and often negotiable. The land is beautiful and completely indifferent to human survival. Dust storms roll across plains without warning. Rivers dry up and return on their own schedule. The people are hard, honest in the way that isolation forces honesty, and deeply skeptical of anyone who arrives clean. Stories are traded like currency around fires. Everyone here is running from something or toward something, and most have stopped distinguishing between the two.

Dunwater Coast is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a world of island chains, open ocean, and port towns that ask no questions because the answers are rarely worth the trouble. Ships are crewed by people running from something — debts, names, entire former lives — and the sea is generous enough to let them. The economy runs on trade, smuggling, and the thin line between the two. Storms arrive fast and leave wreckage. Salt crusts everything. Magistrates govern port towns with pragmatism rather than principle. Pirates and merchants overlap so completely that the distinction is largely a matter of who is telling the story. The water between islands is deep, dark, and occasionally wrong — fishermen speak of things seen beneath the surface that they decline to describe in detail.

Aldenmoor is a world accessible through doors of The Endless. It is a high fantasy continent of ancient kingdoms, deep forests, and magic that predates the memory of any living species. Elves hold grudges measured in centuries. Dwarves hold longer ones measured in geological time. Humans build fast, die young, and leave institutions that outlast their founders. Magic here costs something real — not mana points or spell slots, but years, memories, or pieces of the self that do not grow back. The land is vast and varied: frozen northern reaches, temperate kingdoms of stone and banner, southern jungles where ruins predate the oldest elven records. Gods are real but distant, communicating through intermediaries who disagree about what was said. Wars are fought over territory, succession, and interpretations of prophecy that may have been mistranslated centuries ago.

When portraying NPC dialogue, always use bold name attribution before spoken words: **Name:** "Dialogue here." Use *italics* for narrative action and description. When multiple NPCs are present, attribute every spoken line to a named speaker. Maintain clear separation between narration and dialogue with blank lines. Each speaker change begins on a new line. Intersperse action beats between dialogue lines: **Sable:** "First line." *Action description.* **Wren:** "Response." This format ensures proper chat bubble rendering and character distinction.
